dimensions.what_it_captures: Whether the individual responds more strongly to emotional appeals (e.g., joy, nostalgia) or rational, fact-based messaging in ads and product concepts.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: Product concepts with emotional storytelling (e.g., 'feel refreshed and confident') may resonate more with emotionally-driven individuals, increasing purchase intent, while rational concepts with clinical benefits may appeal more to others.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: Ads that evoke emotions (e.g., heartwarming narratives) may have higher engagement and click-through rates for those high on emotional resonance, whereas ads focusing on facts and expert endorsements may perform better for rational processors.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: From A6 'calm, composed' and A8 'mix of both' for grooming, there are hints of both rational and emotional aspects, but no direct question on ad emotionality, so it remains uncertain.
dimensions.example_variation: Two twins see the same body wash ad: Twin A is moved by the emotional story of renewal and immediately clicks to buy, while Twin B finds the ad fluffy and only engages when shown a version with dermatologist testimonials and lab data.

dimensions.dimension_name: Visual versus Textual Preference in Ad Consumption
dimensions.what_it_captures: The degree to which the individual engages with visual content (images, videos) versus textual information (reviews, descriptions) when encountering ads or product concepts.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: For visual-preferring individuals, product concepts with high-quality imagery or demo videos will be more appealing and increase perceived value; text-heavy concepts may appeal more to those who prefer detailed, written information.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: Visual ads (e.g., Instagram reels, video ads) may capture attention and drive clicks for visual-preferring twins, while text-based ads (e.g., article-style, infographics) may engage textual-preferring twins more effectively.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: A59 includes 'expert_review' and 'before_after', which have textual and visual elements, and A56 'natural content, less flashy' hints at subtle visuals, but no explicit preference is stated, leaving it ambiguous.
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A scrolls past a text-heavy ad but stops to watch a video ad showing the body wash's lather and texture, leading to a purchase; Twin B ignores the video and thoroughly reads a blog-style ad with detailed benefits before considering.

dimensions.dimension_name: Contextual Purchase Triggers for Different Occasions
dimensions.what_it_captures: How the individual's product choice and ad receptivity vary based on specific occasions or contexts, such as workouts, social events, travel, or daily routines.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: Product concepts positioned for specific occasions (e.g., 'post-gym refresh', 'date night luxury') may attract those who value context-driven purchases, increasing willingness to pay, while general concepts may suffice for occasion-agnostic individuals.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: Ads shown in contextually relevant settings (e.g., during sports streaming, on travel blogs) may have higher engagement and conversion for context-sensitive individuals, whereas generic ads may be less effective.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: A27 indicates once-daily showering, but no questions address product usage for different occasions; A10's ideal routine is detailed but not occasion-based, so this dimension is not captured.
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A sees an ad for a body wash during a fitness app and considers it specifically for gym use, leading to a trial; Twin B only evaluates body washes for general daily use and ignores occasion-specific ads.

dimensions.dimension_name: Trust in Aspirational versus Relatable Ad Content
dimensions.what_it_captures: Whether the individual finds advertisements more credible and appealing when they feature aspirational lifestyles (e.g., luxury, success) or relatable, everyday scenarios (e.g., common routines).
dimensions.concept_test_impact: Product concepts that promise an aspirational identity (e.g., 'premium feel', 'elite grooming') may appeal more to aspiration-driven individuals, while concepts emphasizing practicality and relatability may attract others.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: Ads with influencers in lavish settings or celebrities may inspire trust and desire in aspiration-driven twins, while ads featuring ordinary people in familiar situations may build trust and connection in relatability-driven twins.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: A14 shows bias towards international brands, which might indicate aspiration, but A56 prefers 'natural content, less flashy', suggesting relatability, so the preference is unclear from the answers.
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A is persuaded by an ad showing a successful model using the body wash and associates it with a high-status lifestyle, leading to purchase; Twin B trusts an ad featuring a student with a similar routine and finds it more authentic.

dimensions.what_it_captures: The tendency to make quick, impulse purchases after exposure to an ad or concept versus engaging in thorough research and deliberation before buying.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: Concepts with urgency elements (e.g., limited edition, flash sales) may trigger impulse buys for some, increasing immediate conversion, while others may require detailed information, comparisons, and time before deciding.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: Ads with strong, immediate calls-to-action (e.g., 'Buy now with 50% off') may lead to higher click-through and conversion for impulse-prone twins, but may be ignored or bypassed by deliberate deciders who prefer to research first.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: A16 indicates moderate loyalty and A7 comfort level 3 with new brands, suggesting some deliberation, but A23 mentions switching due to ads when already considering, hinting at potential impulsivity, so it's not definitively captured.
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A sees an ad for a new body wash and immediately purchases it online without further research; Twin B sees the same ad, saves it, reads multiple reviews for days, and only buys after confirming benefits and price comparisons.
